content.text: When an agentic AI feature reaches end of life, the way you communicate that retirement determines whether users migrate smoothly or scramble at the last minute. A well-designed feature sunset communication and migration plan gives every affected party — developers, operators, end users — enough time, clarity, and tooling to transition without disruption.
What It Is
A
feature sunset communication plan
is the structured process of announcing, documenting, and guiding users through the removal of a product feature or API. It pairs a
timeline of public notices
with a
migration plan

that maps the deprecated capability to its replacement, including code samples, tooling, and a support window. In agentic AI systems, this covers model version deprecations, API endpoint removals, tool schema changes, and the retirement of entire agent personas or capabilities.
Why It Matters

Agentic systems are deeply integrated: a single deprecated tool call or model version can break automated pipelines, customer-facing assistants, and long-running workflows simultaneously. Without a plan, users discover the sunset through failures in production. A clear communication plan converts a potential outage into a predictable, managed change.
Prevents silent failures
— users learn from the announcement, not from a broken API response.
Reduces support load
— a good migration guide answers questions before they become tickets.
Builds trust
— predictable lifecycle management signals a mature product organization.
Satisfies contractual obligations
— enterprise SLAs often mandate a minimum notice period (commonly 12 months).
Benefits
Users have enough lead time to plan and execute migration in normal sprint cycles rather than emergency sprints.
Migration guides reduce the engineering cost per user from hours of research to minutes of copy-paste.
In-product warnings (API headers, UI banners) catch teams that miss email announcements.
A defined support window lets your team batch support requests rather than handling them indefinitely.
Staged communication reduces the number of users who remain on deprecated paths until the final day.
Trade-offs
Long notice periods keep deprecated infrastructure running longer, increasing maintenance burden.
Parallel support of old and new APIs doubles testing surface for security patches.
Aggressive timelines (less than 3 months) hurt smaller teams with less migration capacity.
Over-communicating minor changes can cause alert fatigue, reducing engagement with truly urgent notices.
Providing migration tooling adds engineering cost up front but saves it many times over in support tickets.
How It Works
A sunset plan moves through four sequential phases, each building on the last:
Phase 1 — Announcement (T-6 months):

Publish a deprecation notice to all channels. Include the exact sunset date, what is being removed, and what replaces it. Send targeted email to owners of API keys and accounts that have called the deprecated feature in the last 90 days.
Phase 2 — Migration Guides (T-4 months):
Publish step-by-step migration documentation with runnable code examples. Add
Deprecation
response headers to the deprecated API so in-code logging catches usage during integration testing.
Phase 3 — Reminders (T-1 month):
Send a final email to all remaining users still calling the deprecated endpoint. Add in-product banners for active users. Post a reminder in community channels.
Phase 4 — Sunset (T-0):
Disable the feature. Return a clear error message with a link to the migration guide. Activate redirects where technically possible. Keep the migration guide live for at least 12 months.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Feature sunset communication timeline showing four phases from announcement to sunset, communication channels, and migration plan components

content.text: The diagram shows the four-phase timeline, the communication channels active in each phase, and the four components of a migration plan: identify affected users, map alternatives, define a support window, and validate and close.
When to Use It
Removing a model version from the API (e.g.,
claude-3-opus-20240229
reaching end of support).
Replacing a REST endpoint with a new versioned API (
/v1/agents
moving to
/v2/agents
Retiring a UI feature that some users have built workflows around.
Deprecating a tool schema that agent prompts reference directly.
Removing a legacy data format or output structure that downstream systems parse.
How to Implement
Step 1: Identify Affected Users with Usage Analytics

import boto3
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

def find_deprecated_api_users(
    log_group: str,
    deprecated_endpoint: str,
    lookback_days: int = 90,
) -> list[dict]:
    """Query CloudWatch Logs Insights for callers of a deprecated endpoint."""
    client = boto3.client("logs", region_name="us-east-1")

    query = f"""
    fields @timestamp, api_key_id, user_id, org_id, @message
    | filter endpoint = '{deprecated_endpoint}'
    | stats count(*) as call_count by api_key_id, user_id, org_id
    | sort call_count desc
    | limit 1000
    """

    start_time = datetime.now() - timedelta(days=lookback_days)
    end_time = datetime.now()

    response = client.start_query(
        logGroupName=log_group,
        startTime=int(start_time.timestamp()),
        endTime=int(end_time.timestamp()),
        queryString=query,
    )
    query_id = response["queryId"]

    import time
    while True:
        result = client.get_query_results(queryId=query_id)
        if result["status"] in ("Complete", "Failed", "Cancelled"):
            break
        time.sleep(1)

    users = []
    for row in result.get("results", []):
        record = {field["field"]: field["value"] for field in row}
        users.append(record)

    return users

# Usage
affected = find_deprecated_api_users(
    log_group="/prod/api/access",
    deprecated_endpoint="/v1/completions",
)
print(f"Found {len(affected)} users calling deprecated endpoint")
Step 2: Add Deprecation Headers to the API
Step 3: Send Targeted Deprecation Emails

import boto3 from email.mime.multipart import MIMEMultipart from email.mime.text import MIMEText def send_deprecation_notice( recipient_email: str, feature_name: str, sunset_date: str, migration_url: str, call_count: int, ) -> None: ses = boto3.client("ses", region_name="us-east-1") subject = f"[Action Required] {feature_name} retires on {sunset_date}" body_html = f"""

Action required by {sunset_date}

Your account made {call_count:,} calls to {feature_name} in the past 90 days.

This feature will be permanently disabled on {sunset_date}. Calls made after this date will return HTTP 410 Gone.

Migration steps

See the full migration guide: {migration_url}

If you need help, reply to this email or open a support ticket.

""" ses.send_email( Source="noreply@example.com", Destination={"ToAddresses": [recipient_email]}, Message={ "Subject": {"Data": subject}, "Body": {"Html": {"Data": body_html}}, }, )
Examples
Example 1: Model Version Deprecation Notice (Python SDK)
Example 2: In-Product Banner via Feature Flag
Real-World Example
Anthropic's deprecation of early Claude model versions follows this playbook. When
claude-instant-1

reached end-of-life, Anthropic published a sunset date on the model list endpoint, added a deprecation notice to the documentation, and provided direct replacements in the model naming table. Enterprise customers on contractual support received individual notices. The API continued returning results until the sunset date, then began returning HTTP 404 with a body containing the replacement model name — eliminating the ambiguity of a silent failure.
Common Mistakes
Announcing the sunset but not the replacement — users know something is going away but not what to use instead.
Sending a single email with no follow-up — users who miss it get no second chance until the feature breaks.
Setting the sunset date on a weekend or holiday — engineers are not monitoring, so failures go undetected for longer.
Silently returning HTTP 200 with empty results after sunset — far worse than a clear HTTP 410 with an explanatory body.
Failing to track which users are still calling the deprecated API at T-1 month — you cannot know whether to extend or proceed.
Migration guides with placeholder code that does not actually run — erodes trust immediately.
Best Practices
Use RFC 8594
Sunset
and
Deprecation
HTTP headers on every deprecated endpoint — monitoring tools pick these up automatically.
Segment your user list by usage volume and reach out to high-volume users individually, not just via broadcast email.
Provide a working migration script, not just documentation — a script users can run against their code base to identify all call sites.
Keep the deprecated endpoint alive in a read-only mode for a grace period after the official sunset for stragglers.
Track migration progress via API usage dashboards and adjust your support resources accordingly.
Publish a public changelog entry so users subscribed to the changelog (not just email) are also notified.
Document the reason for the sunset — teams that understand why appreciate the transparency and feel less blindsided.
Performance and Scalability

The deprecation notice pipeline itself must handle scale if your API has millions of users. Batch email sends via SES or SendGrid with exponential back-off, store notification status in a database so retries do not duplicate sends, and track open and click rates to measure how many users have engaged with the notice. Use background jobs rather than synchronous sends so your web server is not blocked.
Send emails in batches of 1,000 with a 50 ms delay per batch to stay within SES sending limits.
Use idempotency keys on email sends so a job re-run after a failure does not deliver duplicates.
Cache the list of affected API keys in Redis to avoid querying the analytics database on every deprecation middleware check.
Tooling and Ecosystem
RFC 8594
— defines the
Sunset
HTTP header standard used by large APIs (AWS, Stripe, GitHub).
sunset-header NPM package
— middleware that reads a sunset config file and injects headers automatically.
LaunchDarkly / Statsig
— feature flags that gate in-product banners based on usage of the deprecated feature.
Amazon SES / SendGrid
— transactional email providers with delivery tracking and bounce handling.
Statuspage (Atlassian)
— publish planned maintenance and deprecation events visible to subscribed users.
AWS CloudWatch Logs Insights / Datadog
— query API access logs to identify and count callers of deprecated endpoints.
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
Notice Period
Migration Support
User Impact
Structured plan (this guide)
6–12 months
Guides, headers, support window
Low disruption
Redirect only
Any
None — transparent to caller
Zero if API is compatible
Hard cutover (no notice)
None
High — production outages
Short notice (1 month)
1 month
Minimal
Medium — rushed migrations
Version freeze (maintain both)
Indefinite
Full old API maintained
None — but high infra cost
Security and Reliability
Keep the deprecated API behind authentication during the notice period — do not open it to anonymous probing just because it is marked deprecated.
Maintain the rate limits on the deprecated endpoint to prevent abuse in the window between announcement and shutdown.
After sunset, return HTTP 410 Gone (not 404 Not Found) — 410 semantically means "permanently removed" and prevents clients from retrying indefinitely.
Ensure the migration email campaign cannot be spoofed — use DKIM/SPF/DMARC and send only from the registered domain, not a marketing subdomain that users might not recognise.
Archive the deprecated endpoint's access logs before decommissioning the service — you may need them for compliance audits.
Key Takeaways
A feature sunset plan has four phases: announcement, migration guides, reminders, and sunset. The exact timeline depends on impact, but 6 months is a common minimum for developer-facing APIs.
Use usage analytics to identify exactly which users call the deprecated feature, then target them directly — broadcast-only campaigns miss teams that filter email.
In-product signals — API response headers, UI banners, SDK warnings — reach users who miss email, and they surface at the exact moment the user is working with the deprecated feature.
Provide a migration guide with runnable code examples, not just prose — users measure the migration effort in minutes of reading, not hours.
After sunset, return HTTP 410 Gone with an explanatory body pointing to the migration guide — silent failures are far more costly than explicit errors.
Track the percentage of users who have migrated at each phase; extend the timeline if a large cohort is still on the deprecated path at T-1 month.
Build the deprecation notice system (email, headers, banners) as reusable infrastructure — the first sunset is expensive to build, but subsequent sunsets become routine.
